1: New oracle-reception formula. Chapter 33 is structurally pivotal: it reopens the watchman commission of chapter 3 (Ezekiel 33:1-9), revisits the individual accountability principles of chapter 18 (Ezekiel 33:12-20), and is anchored by the news of Jerusalem's fall (Ezekiel 33:21). Chapters 1-32 are primarily judgment; from chapter 33 onward, restoration becomes the dominant register.
2: ben-adam daber el-bnei amecha ('son of man, speak to your people'): the shift from beit Yisrael (house of Israel) to amecha (your people) is subtle but significant — the prophet has a personal covenant relationship with the community to whom he speaks. The watchman (tzofeh) analogy returns from Ezekiel 3:17: a man from among a people, posted at the walls, responsible for alarm. The responsibility is communal but the appointment is individual.
3: ura'ah et-hacherev ba'ah al-ha'aretz ('and he sees the sword coming upon the land'): the watchman's function is perception followed by communication — taqe'a beshofar (blow the trumpet). The shofar as the alarm instrument carries the weight of covenant-calling throughout the Hebrew Bible: the same instrument used for Sinai (Ex Ezekiel 19:16-19), jubilee (Leviticus 25:9), and Joel's eschatological alarm (Joel 2:1). The watchman's trumpet is a prophetic voice.
4: damav be'rosho yihyeh ('his blood will be on his own head'): the legal principle of transferred responsibility — when the alarm is sounded and the person fails to act, the watchman's duty is discharged and the unresponsive person bears their own consequence. The 'blood on the head' idiom (cf. Joshua 2:19; 2 Samuel 1:16; Acts 18:6) is a standard Ancient Near Eastern legal formula for placing responsibility.
5: hu shama et-qol hashofar velo nizhar ('he heard the sound of the trumpet and was not warned'): the paradox of hearing-without-heeding — the ear received the signal but the will did not respond. Damav bo yihyeh ('his blood will be on himself'): the legal accountability is total. The watchman who warns has done everything required; the hearer who ignores bears all responsibility. This creates the framework for Ezekiel's ministry: he sounds the alarm; the response belongs to each individual.
6: vedamav midayav edrosh ('and his blood I will require from the watchman's hand'): the reverse case — the watchman who fails to warn is held responsible for the deaths that result. Midam is here plural (bloods) — the accumulated deaths resulting from the failure to warn. The legal language makes Ezekiel's prophetic ministry a matter of life and death not merely for his audience but for himself: silence is complicity.
7: uven-adam tzofeh natati lecha levet Yisrael ('and you, son of man, I have made you a watchman for the house of Israel'): the application of the general watchman-principle to Ezekiel personally. The repetition of the watchman commission from 3:17 in nearly identical terms at the book's structural midpoint reminds the reader (and Ezekiel) that the initial commission has not expired. With Jerusalem fallen, the prophetic watchman-role continues into the restoration era.
8: be'omri lerasha YHWH mot tamut ('when I say to the wicked man, "Wicked one, you shall surely die"') — the legal formula mot tamut (dying you shall die, a doubled infinitive absolute for certainty). The watchman's failure to speak this warning makes him complicit in the death that results. Damav midayav edrosh: YHWH holds Ezekiel responsible. The weight of the prophetic office is that silence kills.
9: ve'atah ki-hizaharta rasha midarkav leshuv mimenna velo-shav ('but if you warn the wicked man to turn from his way and he does not turn'): the watchman's discharge: if the warning is given and refused, the watchman is free. Bedamav yamut ('in his blood he will die') — his refusal to turn seals his fate; the blood is entirely his own. Ve'atah nafshecha hitzalta ('but you — your life you will have saved'): Ezekiel's personal liberation from legal responsibility when he faithfully warns.
10: ben-adam emor el-beit Yisrael ('son of man, say to the house of Israel'): the exiles' complaint — avoneinu va'chata'teinu aleinu ('our transgressions and our sins are upon us'). The weight metaphor: sin and iniquity as a burden pressing down. Umah niqeh ('how can we live?'): the question of despair — if the covenant curses have fully fallen and there is no reversal, survival is impossible. The question prepares for the stunning divine answer of Ezekiel 33:11.
11: chai-ani ne'um Adonai YHWH im-echpatz bemot harasha ('as I live, declares the Lord YHWH, I do not desire/take pleasure in the death of the wicked'): the most theologically important verse in the chapter — perhaps in the book. The divine oath by YHWH's own life (chai-ani) guarantees its absolute truth. Echpatz (to desire, delight in) — YHWH's chefetz (delight/pleasure) is not judgment but restoration. Ki im-beshuv rasha midarkav vachayah ('but rather in the wicked man turning from his way and living'): the teshuvah-formula — turning (shuv) as the path to life. Shuvu shuvu midarkechem hara'im ('turn! turn from your evil ways!'): the doubled imperative of urgency.
12: tzidqat hatzaddiq lo tatzilenu ('the righteousness of the righteous will not deliver him'): the individual accountability principle (cf. Ezekiel 18) — accumulated prior righteousness does not create a permanent credit balance. Veresha' harasha lo yikashel bo ('and the wickedness of the wicked will not cause him to stumble when he turns from it'): accumulated wickedness does not permanently disqualify. The moral condition at the moment of action determines destiny — not the accumulated record.
13: be'omri latzaddiq chayoh yichyeh ('when I say to the righteous man, "You shall surely live"') — chayoh yichyeh, the doubled infinitive absolute of certainty. The danger named: if the righteous person trusts in the promise and then commits evil — kol-tzidqotav lo tizakarnah ('none of his righteous acts will be remembered'). The divine accounting system is not cumulative but present-tense: the dominant direction of the will at any moment determines covenantal standing.
14: uvea'omri larasha mot tamut veshuv mechata'to ('when I say to the wicked man, "You shall surely die," but he turns from his sin'): the pivot case — the condemned man who turns. What the legal verdict declares (death) is reversed by the turn. Ve'asah mishpat utzedaqah ('and does justice and righteousness'): two terms for the same thing from different angles — mishpat (right legal action) and tzedaqah (righteous moral standing). The doublet covers both legal and relational dimensions of covenant faithfulness.
15: chavol yashuv rasha yashiv ('if the wicked man restores the pledge'): specific acts of restitution — gezelah yashlem (repay what was stolen), bechuqot hachayim halach (walk in the statutes of life). The abstract principle of 'turning' is grounded in specific acts of restitution. Without concrete behavioral change, 'repentance' is merely verbal. The statutes of life (chuqqot hachayim) — Torah as the revealed path to life, not merely rules to obey.
16: kol-chata'av asher chata lo yizakru lo ('all the sins he sinned will not be remembered against him'): the divine forgetting of sin upon genuine repentance. The theological weight here is extraordinary: YHWH actively does not remember — not merely forgives but ceases to count. This anticipates the new covenant formula of Jeremiah 31:34 (velo-ezkor od, 'and I will remember no more'). Mishpat utzedaqah asah chayoh yichyeh: the doublet of justice-righteousness again.
17: va'omru bnei amecha lo yitaken derekh Adonai ('and your people say, "The way of the Lord is not fair/straight"') — the accusation against YHWH's justice. Tiqen (to be right/level/fair): the same accusation from 18:25 and Ezekiel 18:29 — YHWH's ways are said to be unequal (lo-yitqanu). The accusation presupposes a human standard of justice by which YHWH's actions can be judged. Ezekiel's response is to reverse the accusation: it is Israel's ways that are unequal.
18: beshov tzaddiq mitzidqato ve'asah aval vameth bahem ('when a righteous man turns from his righteousness and does wrong, he will die by it'): the principle stated again in its application. The verb meth bahem (dies by them) — the sins become the mechanism of death. The righteous person who turns to evil does not merely face future punishment; the evil itself is lethal, carrying death within it.
19: uveshov rasha miresha'o va'asah mishpat utzedaqah ('and when the wicked man turns from his wickedness and does justice and righteousness'): the symmetrical case. Hu yachieh alehem ('he will live by/because of them'): the righteous acts become the mechanism of life. The verb yichyeh (he will live) answers the desperate question of Ezekiel 33:10: 'how can we live?' — by turning and doing justice and righteousness. The answer is simple, costly, and completely accessible to every individual.
20: va'omarten lo yitaken derekh Adonai ('you say, "The way of the Lord is not equal"') — final repetition of the accusation, now answered definitively. Ish kedrakav ishpot etchem ('I will judge each of you according to his ways'): the individual judgment principle as the seal. YHWH's equality is not abstract impartiality but personal accountability — each person judged by their own path. The defense of divine justice is the affirmation of individual moral responsibility.
21: bayom chamishi le'asarah lachodesh bishnat shtem-esreh ('in the fifth day of the tenth month, in the twelfth year'): the precise date marks the most dramatic moment in the book — the arrival of the survivor from Jerusalem. Ba-elai happalit ('the fugitive/survivor came to me'): palit (one who has escaped) — the technical term for a survivor of catastrophe. Hukkatah Yerushalayim ('Jerusalem has been struck'): the single sentence that is the axis of the entire book. The judgment oracles (1-24) have culminated; the restoration oracles (25-48) now begin their context.
22: veyad YHWH haytah elai ba'erev lifnei bo happalit ('and the hand of YHWH was upon me in the evening before the arrival of the survivor'): YHWH prepares Ezekiel supernaturally the evening before the news arrives — the divine communication precedes the human messenger. Uftach et-pi ('and he opened my mouth'): Ezekiel's mouth had been sealed since Ezekiel 3:26 — the dumbness that marked his prophetic restraint during the judgment period. Now it is opened. The new era of speech begins before the news arrives, signaling that YHWH orchestrates both the silence and the speaking.
23: New oracle following the arrival of the news. The people who remained in the land (not exiled) are addressed — they have appropriated the Abrahamic promise to justify possessing the land while the exiles are gone.
24: yoshvei hecharvot ha'el al-admat Yisrael ('those who live in those ruins on the land of Israel'): the remaining population in the land. Their theological argument: Avraham echad hayah vayyiresh et-ha'aretz ('Abraham was one and he possessed the land'): if the solitary Abraham inherited, how much more do we who are many. The use of Abraham as a precedent is a specific misapplication of the promise — the Abrahamic covenant promised the land to Abraham's descendants, not to those who remain after the faithful are exiled.
25: al-hadam ta'achelu ('you eat on/over blood'): the specific violations of the remaining population — eating with blood (prohibited in Genesis 9:4; Leviticus 17:10-14), idolatry (ve'eineihem ta'su el-gilluleichem), murder (vedam tishpeku), all while claiming the land by inheritance. The combination of covenant violations disqualifies them from the very inheritance they claim. The Abrahamic promise is conditional on covenant faithfulness.
26: amadtem al-charbechem asitem to'evah ('you stand on your sword, you have done abomination'): standing on the sword — living by violence — as a defining characteristic. The acts of violence (murder, sexual violence implied by ish et-eshet re'ehu timme'u), idolatry, and bloodshed are the grounds for disqualification from the inheritance. Possession of land without Torah-faithfulness is illegitimate in the covenant framework.
27: koh tomar lahem ('thus you shall say to them'): the specific judgment on the remnant — those in the ruins will fall by sword, those in the open field to animals, those in strongholds will die by pestilence. The full triad of judgment (sword, pestilence, famine/wild animals) from chapters 5-6 is now applied to the remnant. No category escapes; the completeness of judgment matches the completeness of apostasy.
28: venatatiti et-ha'aretz shemamah umeshimamah ('I will make the land a desolation and a waste'): shemamah umeshimamah — the doubled desolation (cognate words for emphasis). The ga'on uzza Israel ('pride of her strength') will cease — the same phrase used in Leviticus 26:19 in the list of covenant curses. The land itself becomes uninhabited, the ultimate covenant curse (Leviticus 26:33).
29: veyad'u ki-ani YHWH betiteni et-ha'aretz shemamah ('and they will know that I am YHWH when I make the land a desolation'): the refrain-formula again, but now pointing toward future recognition. Even the desolation serves YHWH's revelatory purpose — not merely punishment but the establishment of covenant truth for those who survive to comprehend it.
30: ve'atah ben-adam bnei amecha hamitdabrim becha etzel hakirot ('and you, son of man, your people who talk about you by the walls'): the problem of performative piety — the exiles gathering communally to hear Ezekiel as a kind of entertainment. Ve'omer ish el-achiv ('and each one says to his brother'): word about Ezekiel spreads through the community. Bo-na ushemu ma-haddavar hayotze me'et YHWH ('come and hear what the word is that goes out from YHWH'): the intellectual curiosity about prophetic content without obedient response.
31: uvau elecha kimevo am ('they come to you as a people comes'): the formal gathering of a religious audience. Veyashvu lefanecha ami ('my people sit before you'): they identify as 'my people' — the covenant language is used. Veshamu et-devarecha ('and they hear your words'): hearing (shama') without the covenantal dimension of obedience. Ve'otam lo ya'asu ('but they do not do them'): the hearing-doing gap — what James 1:22-25 calls being 'hearers only.' Ki agavim befithem ('for they are like love-songs on their lips'): Ezekiel's words are aesthetically appreciated as shir agavim (love song) — beautiful language without moral force.
32: vehineka lahem keshir agavim ('and you are to them like a love song'): shir agavim — erotic/romantic song, beautiful but not binding. Yefeh qol umentiv naggen ('beautiful of voice and skilled in playing'): Ezekiel is experienced as an aesthetic performance — appreciated for craft and beauty, not transformed by content. The passage is a devastating analysis of religious consumerism: the audience receives the word as entertainment while rejecting it as command. Ushe'amu et-devarecha ('they hear your words') but ve'otam einam osim ('and they do not do them').
33: uvevoa'ah uvevoa'ah uhinenah ba'ah ('and when it comes — and it will come — then they will know'): the eschatological guarantee — when Ezekiel's prophesied events materialize, the entertainment-hearers will finally know that a prophet was among them. Ki navi hayah betocham ('that a prophet had been in their midst'): the past tense in the prediction — when it comes true, they will say 'there was a prophet among us.' The recognition arrives after, not before, the fulfillment. Truth often convinces only retrospectively.
